coredata.dc:title: Low-dimensional carbon materials decorated FAPbI3 for carbon-based perovskite solar cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Xiang, Wenjun
Gu, Xiaoyu
Khawaja, Kausar
Yuan, Mengmeng
Picart, Christopher
Li, Lin
Yan, Feng
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanomaterials are at the forefront of research in perovskite solar cells (PSCs) due to their exceptional electrical, optical, and stability properties. Their diverse applications include serving as interfacial layers, additives, hole and electron transport materials, and back electrodes. While the influence of various low-dimensional carbon nanomaterial structures on crystallinity, optical and electrical performance, and overall device efficiency has been a topic of interest, it has not been thoroughly explored until now. In this study, we effectively integrated carbon quantum dots (CQDs), multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), and graphene into the FAPbI3 photoactive layer using a two-step sequential deposition method. Our experiments revealed marked improvements in the photovoltaic performance of PSCs that incorporated all three types of carbon nanomaterials. In particular, the data shows significant enhancements in power conversion efficiency, demonstrating the effectiveness of these materials in optimizing device functionality. Notably, MWCNTs distinguished themselves by exhibiting a remarkable potential for enhancing long-term stability. This finding underscores the importance of selecting the right carbon nanomaterials for future PSC developments, paving the way for more reliable and efficient solar energy solutions. Our research highlights the critical role of carbon nanomaterials in advancing perovskite solar technology.
coredata.dcterms:subject: FAPbI3 perovskite
Carbon materials
Two-step deposition
Environmental stability

This material is also based upon work supported by the U.S. Department of Energy's Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy (EERE) under the Solar Energy Technologies Office Award Number DE-EE0009833.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of two-step PVSK with an optical picture of synthesized PVSK films. (b) Optical picture of carbon solution with carbon materials structure (1 mg/ml). (c) Transmittance of control and carbon-modified films.

Surface (a–d) and cross-section (e–h) SEM images of control and CQD, MWCNT and Graphene modified perovskite. (i–l) GIWAXS patterns of control and carbon materials treated FAPbI3 films with incidence angle of 0.2°.

XRD patterns of unencapsulated (a) Control and (b) CQD, (c) MWCNT, (d) Graphene modified perovskite films in ambient as deposit vs after three days. (e) The peak intensity of α-FAPbI3, δ-FAPbI3 and PbI2 as a function of storage days.

(a) J-V curves and (b) EQE spectra of control and carbon materials modified PSCs. Statistical distribution of (c) PCE, (d) V OC, (e) J SC and (f) FF for the control PSC and CQD, MWCNT and graphene modified PSCs.

(a) (b) UPS spectra of control and CQD, MWCNT, graphene modified films. (c) Energy level alignment diagram extracted from the UPS results. Light intensity dependence of (d) V OC and (e) J SC, (c) Dark J–V curves and (d) Nyquist plots of EIS measurement for control and carbon materials treated devices. (e) Steady state PL and (f) TRPL spectra of control PSC and CQD, MWCNT, graphene modified films with ETL.

(a) Operation of unencapsulated devices under MPP conditions with 25 % RH. (b) The stability of unencapsulated devices in ambient with 50 % RH. (c) Photographic images of the color changes of the perovskite films in ambient over time. Contact angle of glass and CQD, MWCNT, graphene-treated glasses (d) and control and CQD, MWCNT and graphene-modified perovskite films (e). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Photovoltaic Parameters of the Control PSCs and CQD, MWCNTs, graphene-modified PSCs. (0.01 mg/ml for carbon nanomaterials).

head.ce:title: Low-dimensional carbon materials decorated FAPbI3 for carbon-based perovskite solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Science and Engineering Program, School for Engineering of Matter, Transport, and Energy, Arizona State University, Tempe, 85287, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School for Engineering of Matter, Transport, and Energy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Materials Science and Engineering Program, School for Engineering of Matter, Transport, and Energy, Arizona State University, Tempe, 85287, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanomaterials are at the forefront of research in perovskite solar cells (PSCs) due to their exceptional electrical, optical, and stability properties. Their diverse applications include serving as interfacial layers, additives, hole and electron transport materials, and back electrodes. While the influence of various low-dimensional carbon nanomaterial structures on crystallinity, optical and electrical performance, and overall device efficiency has been a topic of interest, it has not been thoroughly explored until now. In this study, we effectively integrated carbon quantum dots (CQDs), multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), and graphene into the FAPbI3 photoactive layer using a two-step sequential deposition method. Our experiments revealed marked improvements in the photovoltaic performance of PSCs that incorporated all three types of carbon nanomaterials. In particular, the data shows significant enhancements in power conversion efficiency, demonstrating the effectiveness of these materials in optimizing device functionality. Notably, MWCNTs distinguished themselves by exhibiting a remarkable potential for enhancing long-term stability. This finding underscores the importance of selecting the right carbon nanomaterials for future PSC developments, paving the way for more reliable and efficient solar energy solutions. Our research highlights the critical role of carbon nanomaterials in advancing perovskite solar technology.

ce:section.ce:para: To date, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of perovskite solar cells (PSCs) has soared to a certified value of 26.7 % [1]. Nevertheless, PSCs are plagued by several challenges, including issues with poor film morphology, non-radiative recombination and the degradation of noble metal electrode, which hinder their efficiency and stability performance [2–4]. Carbon nanomaterials exhibit outstanding electrical conductivity and optical properties, along with high chemical and thermal stability [5,6]. Their distinctive structure provides a direct pathway for charge transport, effectively mitigating charge recombination and leakage currents [7]. Furthermore, carbon electrodes are cost-effective and chemically inert, minimizing the likelihood of reacting with halide salts [8]. With ongoing research and development, carbon nanomaterials are expected to play an increasingly pivotal role in advancing the performance of PSCs.

Carbon nanomaterials have been extensively utilized in PSCs including carbon quantum dots, carbon nanotubes, graphene and its derivatives, and other conductive carbon materials. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterials with ultrafine sizes below 10 nm [9]. In recent years, CQDs have attracted surged attention due to their non-toxicity, large surface area, efficacious light harvesting, and outstanding electron transport abilities, making them promising candidates for photovoltaic and optoelectronic devices [10,11]. Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are one-dimensional (1D) carbon nanomaterials with unique hollow cylinder structure [12]. They are renowned for their high optical transparency, high surface area, eminent electrical conductivity, and robust chemical and thermal stability [13,14]. In addition, the inherent hydrophobic nature of CNTs enhances the moisture resistance of the PSC, showcasing promising prospects in photovoltaic applications [15]. Graphene is a two-dimensional (2D) sheet with sp2 hybridized honeycomb lattice structure [16]. Graphene exhibits extraordinarily specific surface area and is highly transparent in visible spectra. Graphene-based materials demonstrate exceptional electrical conductivity, excellent mechanical properties, and economically feasible manufacturing processes [17,18]. Consequently, graphene and its derivatives such as graphene oxide, reduced graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots show significant potential in the realm of photovoltaic devices [19–21].

Carbon nanomaterials have been utilized in various roles within PSCs, including interfacial layers [22–25], electron transfer layers (ETLs) [26–29], photoactive layers [30–33], hole transfer layers (HTLs) [34–37], and electrodes [38–40]. Incorporating carbon nanomaterials into photoactive layers has consistently yielded positive results for PSC performance. Xu et al. incorporated two different carbon quantum dots into CH3NH3PbI3 perovskite via one-step processing. A-CQDs modified PSCs exhibited improved efficiency and stability compared to pristine devices, whereas PSCs with CA-CQD additives displayed inferior performance [30]. Seo et al. integrated single-walled carbon nanotubes as additives into photoactive layer, achieving a significant increase in PCE from 18.1 % to 19.5 %, where CNTs work as both the crystal growth templates and charge bridges [31]. Wang et al. fabricated PSCs with amino-functionalized carbon nanotubes resulting in a champion power conversation efficiency of 21.05 %, with improved crystallization and carrier extraction capabilities [32]. Brominated graphene oxide was synthesized and innovatively applied in CsPbBr3 perovskite as an effective additive by Sun et al. Graphene oxide simultaneously passivated grain boundaries and facilitated the hole transfer, successfully raised the PCE from 6.28 % to 10.10 %, and maintained 90 % of the original efficiency after 30 days aging in air with 85 % RH [33]. However, the distinct impact of different structures of low-dimensional carbon nanomaterials on crystallinity, optical and electrical behavior as well as device performance has not been systematically investigated.

In this work, we conducted a comprehensive analysis of the low dimensional carbon materials in the carbon electrode-based perovskite solar cells. We utilized a two-step sequential deposition method to incorporate carbon quantum dots (CQDs), multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), and graphene into the FAPbI3 perovskite layer during the second deposition step. The two-step sequential deposition approach is recognized for its effectiveness in controlling crystal growth and film morphology [41]. Additionally, we implemented phenethylammonium iodide (PEAI) as a passivation layer between the perovskite film and the HTL. Furthermore, carbon electrode was applied to address the long-term stability concerns associated with metal electrode degradation [42]. Our findings indicated that all three types of carbon nanomaterials contributed to improved photovoltaic performance, with MWCNT-treated PSCs achieving a champion PCE of 17.27 %. Notably, only MWCNTs demonstrated the potential for enhancing long-term stability.

PbI2 (99.999 %, Sigma-Aldrich), formamidinium iodide (FAI, 99.99 %, GreatCell Solar), methylammonium chloride (MACl, GreatCell Solar), carbon quantum dots (CQD, XFNANO), multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs, >95 wt%, Cheaptubes Inc), graphene (XFNANO), SnO2 (15 % in H2O colloidal dispersion, VWR), phenethylammonium iodide (PEAI, Sigma-Aldrich), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99.8 %, Sigma-Aldrich), dimethyl formamide (DMF, 98 %, Sigma-Aldrich), isopropyl Alcohol (IPA, VWR), Spiro-OMeTAD (Sigma-Aldrich), lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide (Li-TFSI, 99.95 %,Sigma-Aldrich), 4-tert-butylpyridine (TBP, Sigma-Aldrich.), chlorobenzene (anhydrous, 99.8 %, Sigma-Aldrich) were used as received without any further purification. Carbon paste was purchased from Guangzhou Saidi technology development Co. Ltd., China.

ITO substrates (1.5 × 1.5 cm) were sonicated with detergent solution, deionized water (DIW), acetone, and isopropanol solution 20 min each step and followed by a 30 min UV-ozone treatment. The 15 % SnO2 colloidal solution was diluted to 2.67 % with DIW. Then the SnO2 precursor was spin-coated on the cleaned ITO substrates at 3000 rpm for 30 s followed by 30 min anneal at 150 °C in ambient. These substrates were then treated by UV for 10 min. In a nitrogen-filled glovebox, the perovskite layer was deposited by a two-step spin-coating process: first PbI2 (1.5 M in DMF: DMSO) precursor was spin coated on the SnO2 layer with 1500 rpm for 30 s and post annealed at 70 °C for 1 min; then the IPA solution containing organic halide materials (FAI: MACl 90: 14 mg/ml) and carbon materials was spin-coated with 1800 rpm for 30 s. Then perovskite films were annealed at 140 °C for 20 min in air with 30 % RH. For passivation interlayer, the PEAI precursor solution was deposited at 3000 rpm for 30 s on the resultant perovskite film. The hole transport layer was formed by spin-coating prepared spiro-MeOTAD solution at 3000 rpm for 30 s. Spiro-OMeTAD precursor solution was prepared by dissolving 72.3 mg of spiro-OMeTAD, 28.8 μL of tBP, and 17.5 μL of Li-TFSI solution (520 mg of Li-TFSI in 1 mL acetonitrile) in 1 mL of chlorobenzene. The carbon electrode was prepared by the solvent exchange technique [43]. In brief, the commercial carbon paste was initially coated onto a glass substrate using a doctor-blading technique. Then the wet carbon film was immersed in ethanol to remove the residual solvent through the solvent-exchange process for about 10 min and then dried by N2 gas flow. Finally, the dry free-standing carbon film with a thickness of about 75 μm was hot-pressed onto perovskite film with Spiro-OMeTAD to form the devices at 80 °C and 100 psi. The active area of the device of 0.08 cm2 was defined using an aperture.

The surface morphology and cross-sectional images of pristine and carbon modified perovskite were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Thermo Scientific Apreo). X-ray diffraction (XRD, 45 kV, 40 mA Cu Kα radiation) and grazing-incidence wide-angle X-ray scattering (GIWAXS, Xenocs Xeuss) were used to measure the crystallization and decomposition for perovskite films. The electrical characterization of PSCs with an active area of 0.08 cm2 was recorded using a solar simulator with a Keithley 2420 source meter under simulated AM 1.5G (100 mW/cm2) solar irradiation. The parameters were measured from −0.2 to 1.2 V with a scan rate of 100 mV s−1. The external quantum efficiency (EQE) and transmittance were obtained by an EnliTech QE measurement system. Bandgap alignments were detected by Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS, Kratos Axis Supra+) with unfiltered H e І (21.12 eV) excitation. The light intensity was calibrated using a silicon reference cell (Newport, 91150V, certified by National Renewable Energy Lab. The EIS measurement was performed using a Solartron Analytical 1260 impedance analyzer at a bias potential of 0.5 V in the dark, with the frequency ranging from 1 Hz to 1 MHz. Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) and Time resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) were obtained by spectrofluorometer with Ozone-free xenon arc lamp and picosecond pulsed diode laser (Edinburgh instruments). The stability was described by Maximum power point (MPP).

Fig. 1 a shows the schematic of the procedures for the two-step deposition methods. Sequential depositions of PbI2 and carbon materials incorporated FAI were carried out via spin-coating, followed by an annealing process. The optical images revealed the color variation from the light-yellowed PbI2 film to the orange-red film after coating FAI with or without carbon materials. The black-phased FAPbI3 formed after the second step annealing process. Optical pictures of the IPA-based carbon material solutions with 1 mg/ml concentration, along with their corresponding chemical structures, are displayed in Fig. 1b. Fig. 1c shows the transmittance spectra of the pristine perovskite film and the perovskite films blended with CQDs, MWCNTs, and graphene, respectively. It is worth noting that all low-dimensional carbon materials can enhance optical transmission properties. Furthermore, the Tauc plot in Fig. S1 illustrates the bandgap of the FAPbI3 perovskite, which is consistent with the literature [44].

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) was exploited for a comprehensive understanding of how carbon materials are incorporated within the perovskite layer. Fig. 2 a–d depicts the top-view images of pristine FAPbI3 perovskite film and perovskite treated with various carbon materials. There is a clear visualization of closely packed crystal grains. Interestingly, the MWCNTs and graphene flakes were observed to be randomly distributed on the perovskite surface, while the CQDs were found to be localized within the grains. Furthermore, it was noteworthy that the incorporation of carbon materials showed minimal impact on the perovskite layer thickness as shown in the cross-sectional images (Fig. 2e–h). Additionally, MWCNTs and graphene were not observed within the bulk perovskite layer. Grazing-incidence wide-angle X-ray scattering (GIWAXS) measurements were further conducted to investigate the impact of carbon materials. The 2D diffraction patterns at incidence angles of 0.2° and 1.0° are presented in Fig. 2 and Fig. S2. Fig. 2i–l confirmed the formation of the α-FAPbI3 perovskite phase, indicated by a diffraction ring at 1 Å⁻1. Peaks at 0.9 and 0.8 Å⁻1 correspond to PbI2 and δ-FAPbI3, respectively [45]. 1D GIWAXS data (Fig. S3) provides more distinct peak positions. Compared to the control, all perovskite films treated with carbon materials exhibited improved crystallization of α-FAPbI3. Notably, no diffraction rings corresponding to δ-FAPbI3 or PbI2 were observed in the MWCNT-modified perovskite films. This observation aligns with our X-ray diffraction (XRD) findings.

Fig. 3 a–d shows X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the unencapsulated perovskite films stored in ambient conditions to gain insight into the influence of carbon materials on the perovskite crystal structure. The peaks at 14°, 24.3°, 28.1°, and 31.4° are strong evidence of the presence of cubic structure α-FAPbI3, while the peaks at 11.8°, 26°, and 12.8° can be assigned to δ-FAPbI3 phase and PbI2 respectively [46,47]. Remarkably, after three days of exposure to ambient conditions, it was observed that the peak intensity of δ-FAPbI3 (11.8°) and PbI2 (12.8°) were noticeably upsurged in CQD-modified and graphene-modified perovskite films. Conversely, MWCNTs-modified perovskite film exhibited a smaller rise in δ-FAPbI3 (11.8°) and a milder decrease of α-FAPbI3 (14°) compared with control film. Fig. 3e illustrates the characteristic peak intensity of α-FAPbI3, δ-FAPbI3 and PbI2 as a function of storage days. Specifically, the MWCNT-modified perovskite film demonstrated inhibited phase transitions from α-FAPbI3 to δ-FAPbI3 and minimized the formation of PbI2. This reinforces the superior moisture resistance of MWCNTs, while both CQDs and graphene adversely affect stability performance.

A possible reason for the exceptional stability of the MWCNT-modified film is the hydrophobic nature of MWCNTs. MWCNTs are cylindrical carbon nanomaterials consisting of multiple layers of graphene sheets with sp2 hybridized structure [15]. This hydrophobicity may be advantageous in shielding the perovskite film from moisture. In contrast, CQDs are typically synthesized to be hydrophilic to enhance their dispersion in water or polar solvents [48], which may contribute to faster degradation. Graphene is generally hydrophobic due to its sp2 hybridized carbon structure [49]. However, the incorporated graphene nanoflakes may disrupt the grain boundaries and compromise perovskite stability. Fig. 6 offers additional evidence supporting this observation.

The current density-voltage (J-V) curves are presented in Fig. 4 a. The incorporation of a moderate amount of CQD, MWCNTs, or graphene into the PSCs results in a significant enhancement of both J SC and V OC. The improved J SC values can be attributed to the excellent optical properties and high electrical conductivity of the carbon materials [5,6]. Among all the modifications, MWCNTs-modified PSCs achieved the highest J SC. The increased V OC can be explained by the high charge transfer rate of carbon materials, which can mitigate the nonradiative recombination [7]. Notably, MWCNTs-modified PSCs exhibited the most substantial improvement in V OC. Due to the unique hollow cylinder structure, when MWCNTs are embedded into the grain boundaries, they act as carrier transport channels between perovskite and HTL. Also, they can serve as charge bridges at grain boundaries, improving the mobility of the perovskite [32]. As shown in Fig. 4b, the enhanced EQE observed in the carbon nanomaterials modified device indicates efficient photon-to-electron conversion results from significantly improved charge carrier extraction and transport, along with reduced recombination losses. The integrated photocurrent densities (J EQE) for all devices are slightly lower than those derived from J-V curves. The divergence between J EQE curve and J-V curve may be due to no pre-conditioning for EQE measurement. The device performance statistical distributions are illustrated in Fig. 4c–f. The detailed photovoltaic parameters are listed in Table 1 . It is notable that all three low-dimensional carbon nanomaterials had a strongly positive impact on the overall device performance. Here we also conduct research with varying concentrations of carbon materials (Fig. S4).

Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) was used to characterize pristine and carbon materials modified films. The secondary-electron cut-off energy (Fig. 5 a) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) binding energy (Fig. 5b) determined the position of the valence band maximum (VBM). Combined with the corresponding band gap data (Fig. 1c and Fig. S1), an accurate energy diagram was constructed (Fig. 5c). It is evident that incorporating carbon materials into perovskite absorber layer benefits carrier extraction. Fig. 5d shows the relationship between V OC and light intensity. According to
V
o c
=
n
k B
T
q
l n I + c o n s t a n t
, where
K B
is the Boltzmann constant, T is the temperature in Kelvin, q is the elementary charge,
I
is the light intensity and n is the ideality factor. The slope of the fitting line represents the value of nK
B
T/q, with n values close to 1 indicating reduced defect-assisted recombination [50]. The result revealed that the introduction of carbon materials into the perovskite layer reduced the ideality factor (1< MWCNT < CQD < Graphene < Control), indicating the suppression of nonradiative recombination. Fig. 5e shows the dependence of J SC on light intensity, expressed as
J
s c
=
I α
, where
I
is the light intensity and α represents the exponential factor. The carbon modified PSCs showed an α value closer to 1 (Control < Graphene < CQD < MWCNT <1), suggesting a better charge transfer mobility [51]. Fig. 5f demonstrated the dark J-V curves of all types of PSCs. All carbon materials modified PSCs showed an effectively eliminated leakage current corresponding to the reduced charge nonradiative recombination [52]. The EIS spectra are displayed in Fig. 5g. A recombination resistivity ranking of MWCNT > CQD > Graphene > Control can be readily observed, indicating the efficacious inhibited carrier recombination with incorporated carbon materials [53]. Meanwhile, the results of steady-state photoluminescence (PL) and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) also revealed the carrie extraction efficiency was improved by carbon materials. As presented in Fig. 5h, the stronger quenching effect on the PL of carbon modified films suggests an enhanced carrier extraction at the perovskite/ETL interface. Fig. 5i exhibits fitting curves of the dynamic TRPL data. This shorter lifetime of films with carbon materials indicated reduced carrier recombination due to the more efficient carrier extraction and transport.

The long-term stability of PSCs is an important factor. The maximum power point (MPP) tracking under 1 sun illumination in dry air (∼25 % humidity, RT) was characterized as shown in Fig. 6a. The MWCNT modified PSC displayed superior stability, maintaining 95 % of its initial efficiency after 5 h of continuous operation. While the control device only remained 87 % of its initial efficiency after same duration. By contrast, the CQD and graphene modified PSC dropped significantly to <80 % of their original value. The significantly enhanced stability of MWCNT-treated PSCs can be attributed to the hydrophobic nature of MWCNTs, which is consistent with XRD results. The long-term stability of unencapsulated PSCs under ambient storage (∼50 % humidity, RT) was also conducted as illustrated in Fig. 6b. The same decay trend demonstrates the good reliability of the testing. Fig. 6c displays optical images depicting the color changes in perovskite films under ambient storage conditions. Films modified with carbon quantum dots and graphene showed accelerated degradation compared to the control sample, whereas the MWCNT-treated film demonstrates superior stability. Fig. 6d shows the contact angle of glass and CQD, MWCNT, graphene-treated glasses. The results clearly demonstrate the hydrophilic properties of CQD and the hydrophobic nature of graphene and MWCNT, consistent with existing literature. However, the contact angle of pristine perovskite and CQD, MWCNT, graphene-modified perovskite films (Fig. 6e) indicate that the incorporation of graphene reduced the moisture resistance of perovskite, which may be attributed to the disruption of grain boundaries caused by the graphene nanoflakes.

Our study has shown that incorporating carbon quantum dots (CQDs), multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), and graphene into the FAPbI3 perovskite layer using a two-step sequential deposition method effectively enhances the efficiency of PSCs. The excellent optical and electrical properties of these carbon materials likely played a crucial role. Specifically, the carbon electrode-based MWCNT-modified PSCs exhibited a noticeable enhancement in PCE thanks to the unique one-dimensional hollow cylindrical structure, which serves as carrier transport bridges between perovskite and HTL as well as at grain boundaries. However, in terms of stability performance, only MWCNTs demonstrated a positive impact, due to the joint influence of hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity of carbon nanomaterials. Among all carbon materials, the MWCNT-modified PSC exhibited the highest performance, retaining 95 % of its initial efficiency.

Wenjun Xiang: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Xiaoyu Gu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Kausar Khawaja: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Mengmeng Yuan: Methodology. Christopher Picart: Methodology. Lin Li: Writing – review & editing. Feng Yan: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This material is based upon work supported by the National Science Foundation (NSF) Under Grant No. DMR-2330738, CMMI-2226918, TI-2329871, and ECCS-2413632. This material is also based upon work supported by the U.S. Department of Energy's Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy (EERE) under the Solar Energy Technologies Office Award Number DE-EE0009833.
